session_title: HPV Vaccination and Self-Sampling for Cervical Cancer Prevention and Early Detection Globally and in Underserved Settings
talk_title: Single-Dose HPV Vaccination Efficacy and Immunogenicity

speaker.name: Amy Kramer
speaker.affiliation: National Cancer Institute, Intramural Research Program, Division of Cancer Epidemiology and Genetics

biomarkers.name: HPV 16/18 infection
biomarkers.finding: One-dose regimen achieved comparable protection against incident HPV 16/18 infection as three-dose regimen in ESCUDO trial

models.name: ESCUDO
models.genotype_or_key_features: Girls 12-16 years, randomized to one vs two doses of Cervarix (bivalent) or Gardasil 9 (nonavalent)

models.name: PRISMA
models.genotype_or_key_features: Women 18-30 years, single-dose bivalent or nonavalent vs control

MOA_summary: A single dose of prophylactic HPV L1 virus‑like particle vaccine induces durable neutralizing antibodies that are four‑fold lower than three‑dose levels yet provide comparable protection against incident HPV 16/18 infection, demonstrating non‑inferiority to the standard two‑dose schedule in large randomized trials.
key_findings: One‑dose HPV vaccination showed durable antibody levels up to 11 years post‑vaccination with only a four‑fold reduction versus three‑dose levels.
ESCUDO randomized trial (≈20,000 girls) met co‑primary non‑inferiority endpoints for both Cervarix and Gardasil 9, demonstrating >80% efficacy for one‑dose versus two‑dose schedules.
Global HPV vaccine uptake in 2020 was only 20% overall and ~9% in low‑resource settings, but one‑dose schedules are now adopted in ~90 countries, raising one‑dose coverage to 63%.
Meta‑analyses of single‑dose trials (Kenya, Tanzania, India) confirm high efficacy against virologic and histologic endpoints across vaccine platforms.
open_questions: Efficacy of a single dose in adult women and men, and at non‑cervical sites (oropharynx, anal).
Long‑term durability beyond 20 years and correlates of protection.

session_title: HPV Vaccination and Self-Sampling for Cervical Cancer Prevention and Early Detection Globally and in Underserved Settings
talk_title: Results from the NCI SHIP Trial: Self‑Collected HPV Testing Accuracy

biomarkers.name: High‑risk HPV detection
biomarkers.finding: Positive percent agreement with clinician collection was 92% overall; 94% for HPV‑16, 89% for HPV‑16/18, 89.5% for other 12 types

models.name: SHIP trial (SO1 sub‑protocol, BD/Waters Onclarity)
models.genotype_or_key_features: 2,088 enrolled participants across 24 US sites; 495 completed per‑protocol; diverse demographic (23.6% Hispanic, 37.6% Black)

MOA_summary: Self‑collected vaginal HPV samples processed with the FDA‑approved Onclarity assay demonstrate >92% positive percent agreement with clinician‑collected samples and high sensitivity for CIN 2/3 detection, supporting regulatory approval of home‑based HPV testing.
key_findings: The SHIP trial enrolled 2,088 participants across 24 US sites, with 495 completing the SO1 protocol.
Self‑collected vaginal samples achieved 89.2% sensitivity for CIN 3+ (vs 96.9% for clinician samples) and 83.7% for CIN 2+ (vs 90.7%).
Positive percent agreement between self‑collection and clinician collection was 92% for overall high‑risk HPV, 94% for HPV‑16, and ~89% for other types.
The FDA granted the first pre‑market approval for a self‑collection HPV assay (BD/Waters Onclarity) based on these data.
The trial population was diverse (23.6% Hispanic, 37.6% Black) and included 42% previously vaccinated, supporting generalizability to underserved groups.
open_questions: How will the remaining sub‑protocols (Roche, Abbott) perform in terms of accuracy and regulatory approval?
What implementation strategies will maximize uptake and follow‑up in safety‑net settings?

session_title: HPV Vaccination and Self-Sampling for Cervical Cancer Prevention and Early Detection Globally and in Underserved Settings
talk_title: Implementation of HPV Self‑Sampling in Safety‑Net Settings

speaker.name: Jane Montealegre
speaker.affiliation: University of Texas MD Anderson Cancer Center, Associate Professor of Behavioral Science

models.name: PRESTESS trial
models.genotype_or_key_features: 2,474 under‑screened women in Harris Health System, randomized to usual care vs mailed self‑collection kit vs kit + navigation

models.name: HOME trial
models.genotype_or_key_features: Kaiser Northwest, mailed kits to under‑screened women

MOA_summary: Deploying mailed or clinic‑based HPV self‑collection kits, combined with patient navigation, markedly increases screening participation among underserved women, demonstrating that implementation strategy—not assay choice—is the primary driver of reach.
key_findings: Mailed self‑collection kits in the PRESTESS trial doubled screening participation (47% vs 21% usual care) among under‑screened safety‑net patients.
Adding patient navigation to mailed kits further improved uptake (41% vs 21%).
Meta‑analyses show self‑sampling yields 2‑2.5‑fold higher participation compared with clinician‑collected screening in underserved populations.
Implementation context (mail, door‑to‑door, clinic integration) drives effectiveness more than the specific HPV assay used.
Cost‑effectiveness analyses indicate self‑sampling is highly sensitive to implementation design, with navigation and high return rates essential for value.

combination_strategies.rationale: Addresses logistical, financial, and trust barriers to follow‑up after a positive self‑sample
combination_strategies.phenotype: 2‑fold increase in screening uptake versus usual care

combination_strategies.rationale: Reduces need for clinic visit, increasing accessibility for under‑screened women
combination_strategies.phenotype: 2‑2.5× higher participation compared with standard outreach

open_questions: How can door‑to‑door outreach be sustainably funded in safety‑net health systems?
What are the most effective models for ensuring timely follow‑up (colposcopy, treatment) after a positive self‑sample?
